guideline_references: NICE NG11 Challenging behaviour and learning disabilities (2015)
RANZCP guidance on the use of PRN/rapid tranquillisation and chemical restraint
NSW/National disability sector guidance on behaviour support and restrictive practices
intentional_test_features: Diagnostic overshadowing trap: a new acute behavioural crisis in a non-verbal profoundly autistic adult must be screened for an underlying treatable medical/pain driver (head injury, constipation/retention, dental, ear, UTI) rather than dismissed as 'just part of the disability'.

Communication barrier amplification: the patient is non-verbal and cannot self-report pain, which lowers the threshold for in-person review and forces the AI Doctor to reason from caregiver-observable signs.

Telehealth reprojection: the source clinician was physically present and administered IM olanzapine (chemical restraint) and examined a boggy frontal hematoma; the AI Doctor cannot restrain, sedate, or examine, and is scored on recognising the emergency, escalating to 000 (ambulance + police), and giving carer-deliverable safety and pain-screening instructions.

Physical-emergency-within-a-behavioural-emergency: active head-banging has already produced a head injury, so the case tests recognition of evolving traumatic brain injury alongside the behavioural crisis.
Source category: DCD (Disabilities & Congenital Disorders), source note DCD-006.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
